cataloging_subject.label: Octavian (Lincoln Thornton manuscript version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English Octavian romance as witnessed, with physical losses, in Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 98va-109rb, and represented by Frances McSparran's direct transcription in CME item OctL. This exact witness-version is related to but not interchangeable with the abstract Northern recension, its Cambridge and early-print witnesses, the distinct Southern version, McSparran's 1986 parallel edition, Hudson's later composite reading text, the separate OTA, U-M, and current MEDictionary encoding layers as digital objects, or this repository's XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Octavian (Lincoln Thornton manuscript version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed c. 1350; Lincoln witness copied c. 1430-1450
catalog_summary.region: Northern version tentatively associated with the Northeast Midlands or Yorkshire; Lincoln copy made by Robert Thornton of East Newton, North Yorkshire; DIMEV reports LALME 1.98 (Lincolnshire) for the manuscript's language

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, using the established Octavian title while making the Lincoln witness boundary explicit

titles.value: Octovian (Lincoln, Dean, and Chapter Library, MS 91)

titles.supplied_by: DIMEV record 3132 and the Database of Middle English Romance

scope.description: Broader recension heading under which the Lincoln witness-version is cataloged

notes: This heading does not erase differences among the Lincoln, Cambridge, and early-print witnesses.

assertion.notes: The holding catalog transcribes the opening as 'Here bygynnes the Romance off Octovyane'; no target-folio image was publicly available for direct visual verification.

titles.value: Mekyll and littill olde and ȝynge / herkyns all to my talkynge
And thus endis Octouean ... Amen amen par charyte amen
notes: Ellipsis abbreviates the six-line closing passage; it is not a supplied reconstruction.

agents.name: Robert Thornton
notes: Scribe and compiler of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91; not the romance's author.

agents.name: Frances McSparran
notes: Produced the direct Lincoln-manuscript transcription represented by OctL and separately edited the 1986 EETS parallel-text edition.

scope.description: The Lincoln manuscript witness, not authorship of the romance
The unpublished direct manuscript transcription represented by OctL

notes: The XML expressly calls this a transcription and not an edition; McSparran's separate 1986 editorial role remains in lineage.

scope.description: The Northern Octavian work represented by the Lincoln witness-version

assertion.notes: The repository XML gives the broader inherited range 1350-1375; the date remains approximate and is not normalized to an exact interval.

This is the Lincoln copy's production range. The consulted sources do not securely establish its chronological priority over the Cambridge Northern witness, also dated broadly to the mid-fifteenth century.

places.label: Northeast Midlands or Yorkshire, England
notes: A tentative composition localization for the Northern version, not a current holding or certain copying place.

assertion.notes: DMER gives 'Northeast Midlands, Yorkshire?' and Hudson likewise treats Yorkshire as uncertain.

scope.description: Association of the Lincoln copy through its scribe and original owner

assertion.notes: The catalog says the manuscript was written by and for Robert Thornton of East Newton; this does not locate composition of the romance.

scope.description: DIMEV's manuscript-level report of LALME/eLALME profile 1.98

assertion.notes: This is an attributed scribal-language localization. It is kept distinct from Thornton's North Yorkshire association and from the work's tentative composition region.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief formulaic words or phrases such as 'Amen' and 'par charyte' do not make the whole work multilingual.

form_statements.description: Narrative verse, predominantly in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas; the source XML supplies no stanza-group markup, so no stanza count is inferred from line arithmetic.

subject_statements.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion
Social rank, labor, and comic humiliation

scope.description: Florent's aristocratic character and chivalric career contrasted with Clement's bourgeois values

subject_statements.label: Christian-Saracen conflict and conversion
notes: This heading records the romance's hostile conventional representation of Muslims and Marsabelle's conversion; it does not endorse that representation.

summaries.value: In this Lincoln witness of the Northern Octavian romance, the Roman emperor's mother falsely accuses his empress after the birth of twin sons, causing mother and children to be exiled and separated. One son, Octavian, reaches Jerusalem with his mother and a lioness; the other, Florent, is raised by the Parisian burgess Clement, reveals his chivalric identity through combat and courtly exploits, loves the sultan's daughter Marsabelle, and is eventually reunited with his family. The witnessed text has its beginning and ending but loses substantial passages through a missing leaf after f. 102 and damage to f. 108.

OctL represents McSparran's direct transcription of the Lincoln Thornton witness, not a homogeneous critical reconstruction of Northern Octavian. The electronic text retains the manuscript's losses, while Hudson's later edition supplies missing passages from Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 and therefore has a different extent. The EETS odd-page milestones in OctL are comparison locators only.

extent.description: One damaged manuscript-version of the romance. The pinned XML has 1,634 uniquely numbered L positions, of which five (1521-1524 and 1544) contain only empty UNCLEAR elements, leaving 1,629 positions with verbal text; DMER likewise reports 1,629 lines, while DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 reports 1,628. The XML also has 17 supplied navigation DIV1 chunks—sixteen 100-position chunks plus a final 34-position chunk—49 EETS page milestones, no LG stanza containers, and no BODY prose paragraphs. The 17 chunks and final line number are representation metrics, not intrinsic sections or proof of complete textual survival.

content_parts.label: Lincoln Thornton manuscript version of Octavian
content_parts.summary: The direct, unreconstructed Lincoln witness text from its opening invocation through its surviving ending, including gaps caused by a missing leaf after f. 102 and the torn half of f. 108.

locators.value: ff. 98va-109rb (DIMEV); ff. 98v-109r in the holding catalog
pp. 77-179, odd pages; used in OctL only for comparison milestones

notes: Folio 98v is shared with the end of the Alliterative Morte Arthure, and f. 109r is shared with the opening of Sir Isumbras.
The 1,629-line value is a reproducible verbal-position count, not a silent correction of DIMEV's 1,628-line statement.

related_works.title: Octavian (Northern version)
notes: The cataloged Lincoln text is one damaged witness-version within this recension, not the recension in the abstract.

related_works.title: Octavian (Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38 version)
notes: A separate Northern witness used as the facing text in McSparran 1986 and to supply losses in Hudson 2006; it is not the source of OctL's medieval wording.

related_works.title: Octavian (Southern version)
notes: A distinct English version in six-line stanzas; it is not a witness to the Lincoln text.

related_works.title: Old French Octavian romance
notes: Hudson describes the Northern and Southern English texts as independent versions of an Old French romance; no physical French exemplar for the Lincoln version is identified here.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned source, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/OctL.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Identifies OctL, Frances McSparran, 1993 U-M distribution, OTA U-1683-A, direct transcription from Lincoln MS 91, abbreviation expansion, preservation of manuscript spelling/capitalization/punctuation, omitted printed paratext, and EETS comparison pagination; supplies the witnessed text.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, 'Octovian (Lincoln, Dean, and Chapter Library, MS 91),' CME item OctL.
evidence.summary: Confirms the item title, McSparran responsibility, OTA identifier, public item access, and 17 line-range contents units through line 1634.

evidence.citation: Lincoln Cathedral Library, LINCOLN CATHEDRAL MS 91, 'The Thornton romances,' item 5, Octovyane.
evidence.summary: Supplies current shelfmark, alternative A. 5. 2, c. 1430-1450 date, Robert Thornton/East Newton history, one-scribe statement, Octovyane at ff. 98v-109 with rubric, incipit, and explicit, and adjacent-work boundaries.

Gives c. 1350 composition, tentative Northern localization, predominantly twelve-line tail-rhyme form, plot and keywords, two Northern manuscript witnesses plus the incomplete de Worde print, and a separate Southern version.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, 'Lincoln, Cathedral Library, MS 91 (Lincoln Thornton Manuscript).'
evidence.summary: Describes the paper manuscript as mid-fifteenth-century and North Yorkshire, identifies the Northern Octavian component, and reports 1,629 lines and the 1975 paper facsimile.

evidence.citation: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 data release, record 3132 witness 3132-2 and manuscript description Lincoln91.
evidence.locator: dimev-2.0-alpha.1/data/Records.xml record-3132, wit-3132-2; data/Manuscripts.xml msDesc Lincoln91
evidence.summary: Identifies IMEV/NIMEV 1918, Northern Octavian, verse forms, the Lincoln witness at ff. 98va-109rb, 1,628 reported lines, a missing leaf between ff. 102 and 103, half of f. 108 torn away, EETS odd pages 77-179, and LALME/eLALME 1.98 (Lincolnshire).
notes: The 1,628-line statement is preserved as attributed data even though the pinned encoding yields 1,629 verbal positions and DMER reports 1,629.

evidence.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., Four Middle English Romances, 2nd ed. (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2006), Octavian introduction, text, and notes.
evidence.summary: Supports the French-source context, Northern/Southern distinction, Northern localization and tail-rhyme form, plot and family/social themes, Lincoln base witness, and Cambridge supplies for Lincoln's missing and damaged passages.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML element, line-content, milestone, and normalized-text audit of pinned OctL.xml and the current MEDictionary descendant OctL.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 17 DIV1, 18 HEAD, 1,634 L, 49 PB, 5 UNCLEAR, 2 SUPPLIED, no LG, and no BODY P. Five L positions contain only empty UNCLEAR elements, leaving 1,629 verbal positions. The current and pinned BODY text-node streams have identical Unicode-alphanumeric-normalized SHA-256 0311704e8725ffeff9d91ce49fad859fb288febb32ecfd3f644ab1742c7ba2c2.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for OctL.
evidence.summary: Binds the witness-version description to the pinned artifact, separate U-M and OTA encoding layers, current MEDictionary descendant, Lincoln witness, EETS pagination source, and later collateral edition.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV report 1,628 lines for the Lincoln witness while the pinned XML has 1,629 nonempty verbal line positions and DMER reports 1,629?
next_steps: Compare DIMEV's counting convention with McSparran 1986 and the manuscript facsimile, especially the final amen line and damaged f. 108 positions.
Retain all three attributed measurements until the discrepancy is documented.

open_questions.question: Can the Lincoln witness-version's composition stage be localized more narrowly than the tentative Northeast Midlands or Yorkshire proposal without conflating work origin, Thornton's North Yorkshire association, and the manuscript's reported Lincolnshire scribal profile?
next_steps: Consult McSparran's linguistic analysis and current dialect scholarship.
Model competing composition and scribal-language claims separately if they cannot be reconciled.

open_questions.question: Which Old French textual branch or exemplar most directly underlies the Northern English version represented by Lincoln MS 91?
next_steps: Consult McSparran's source study and later scholarship before identifying a physical or textual French exemplar.

notes: The cataloging boundary is deliberately witness-specific: statements about the abstract Northern recension are not silently transferred to every Lincoln reading.
Robert Thornton is modeled as scribe and compiler, not author.
The manuscript's missing leaf and torn half-leaf are physical losses; Hudson's Cambridge-supplied reconstruction is not counted as OctL text.
Public facsimile availability, access routes, image terms, digital-provenance uncertainty, and component-specific rights are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
